frames.caption: Initialize jumps = 0, curEnd = 0, farthest = 0. Goal is index 4; loop runs i = 0..3 (the last index needs no jump from it).
frames.note: Two scalars carry the whole BFS: `curEnd` is the boundary of the current jump’s reach (the edge of the present level), and `farthest` is how far the present level can collectively reach (the next frontier). `jumps` counts levels. Maintaining just these three ints — instead of an O(n) minJumps[] DP table — is what makes this O(n) time and O(1) space.

frames.caption: i = 0: farthest = max(0, 0 + 2) = max(0, 2) = 2. This level can now reach index 2.
frames.note: Before deciding anything, record how far the CURRENT level can reach: from index i you may jump up to nums[i] steps, so a candidate reach is i + nums[i]. We keep the running MAX across every index in this level — `farthest` is "of all indices reachable in the jumps spent so far, what is the furthest any of them can fling us next?". This is exactly the BFS frontier: the union of all next-hop neighbours of the current layer, summarized as a single high-water mark instead of an explicit queue.

frames.caption: i == curEnd (0): the current jump's range is exhausted — spend a jump. jumps = 1, curEnd advances to farthest = 2.
frames.note: When i reaches `curEnd` we have walked to the very edge of what the last jump could reach — to go any further we MUST spend another jump. So jumps++ and advance the boundary to `farthest`, the furthest the whole exhausted level could reach. That boundary jump is one BFS level transition: everything in (old curEnd, new curEnd] is the next layer, reachable in exactly one more jump. Greedy is safe because no other strategy could be further ahead after the same number of jumps than this maximal `farthest`.

frames.caption: i = 1: farthest = max(2, 1 + 3) = max(2, 4) = 4. This level can now reach index 4.
frames.note: Before deciding anything, record how far the CURRENT level can reach: from index i you may jump up to nums[i] steps, so a candidate reach is i + nums[i]. We keep the running MAX across every index in this level — `farthest` is "of all indices reachable in the jumps spent so far, what is the furthest any of them can fling us next?". This is exactly the BFS frontier: the union of all next-hop neighbours of the current layer, summarized as a single high-water mark instead of an explicit queue.

frames.caption: i (1) < curEnd (2): still inside the current jump's reach — no jump needed, keep walking the level.
frames.note: i is still strictly inside `curEnd`, so the current jump already reaches index i — no new jump is needed to stand here. We only fold its reach into `farthest` and keep walking. Spending a jump now would be premature: BFS does not start a new level until it has drained the current one, and `curEnd` is precisely that "drain the layer first" boundary.

frames.caption: i = 2: farthest = max(4, 2 + 1) = max(4, 3) = 4. This level can now reach index 4.
frames.note: Before deciding anything, record how far the CURRENT level can reach: from index i you may jump up to nums[i] steps, so a candidate reach is i + nums[i]. We keep the running MAX across every index in this level — `farthest` is "of all indices reachable in the jumps spent so far, what is the furthest any of them can fling us next?". This is exactly the BFS frontier: the union of all next-hop neighbours of the current layer, summarized as a single high-water mark instead of an explicit queue.

frames.caption: i == curEnd (2): the current jump's range is exhausted — spend a jump. jumps = 2, curEnd advances to farthest = 4.
frames.note: When i reaches `curEnd` we have walked to the very edge of what the last jump could reach — to go any further we MUST spend another jump. So jumps++ and advance the boundary to `farthest`, the furthest the whole exhausted level could reach. That boundary jump is one BFS level transition: everything in (old curEnd, new curEnd] is the next layer, reachable in exactly one more jump. Greedy is safe because no other strategy could be further ahead after the same number of jumps than this maximal `farthest`.

frames.caption: i = 3: farthest = max(4, 3 + 1) = max(4, 4) = 4. This level can now reach index 4.
frames.note: Before deciding anything, record how far the CURRENT level can reach: from index i you may jump up to nums[i] steps, so a candidate reach is i + nums[i]. We keep the running MAX across every index in this level — `farthest` is "of all indices reachable in the jumps spent so far, what is the furthest any of them can fling us next?". This is exactly the BFS frontier: the union of all next-hop neighbours of the current layer, summarized as a single high-water mark instead of an explicit queue.

frames.caption: i (3) < curEnd (4): still inside the current jump's reach — no jump needed, keep walking the level.
frames.note: i is still strictly inside `curEnd`, so the current jump already reaches index i — no new jump is needed to stand here. We only fold its reach into `farthest` and keep walking. Spending a jump now would be premature: BFS does not start a new level until it has drained the current one, and `curEnd` is precisely that "drain the layer first" boundary.

frames.caption: Loop ends at i = 3; index 4 is already within curEnd = 4. Minimum jumps to reach the last index = 2.
frames.note: The loop stopped one short of the goal because the last index needs no jump out of it — only one INTO it, which an earlier level already paid for. The answer is the number of BFS levels crossed: each `i == curEnd` boundary cost exactly one jump, and greedy’s maximal `farthest` guarantees no schedule of jumps could reach the end in fewer. Path here: 0 → 1 → 4, two jumps.
